question: Explain the Church-Turing thesis and its significance in the theory of computation.
expectedPoints: States that any function that can be computed by an algorithm can be computed by a Turing machine.
Provides a formal, mathematical definition of what it means for a function to be computable.
Serves as a foundational concept that unifies various models of computation (e.g., lambda calculus, recursive functions).

question: Define the Halting Problem and state why it is undecidable.
expectedPoints: The Halting Problem is the problem of determining, from a description of a program and an input, whether the program will finish running or continue to run forever.
It is undecidable because no general algorithm exists that can solve the problem for all possible program-input pairs.
Alan Turing proved its undecidability by showing that assuming a solver exists leads to a logical contradiction (e.g., a program that halts if it doesn't and doesn't halt if it does).

question: Describe the classes P, NP, and NP-complete. Explain the relationship between them and the significance of the P vs NP problem.
expectedPoints: P is the class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
NP is the class of decision problems verifiable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time (or solvable by a non-deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time).
NP-complete is a subset of NP; problems in this class are the 'hardest' in NP, meaning any problem in NP can be reduced to an NP-complete problem in polynomial time.

The relationship is P ⊆ NP, but it is unknown if P = NP. Solving P vs NP would determine whether every problem that can be verified quickly can also be solved quickly, with profound implications for cryptography, optimization, and computer science.
